title: Fun with Rhyming Poems
subject: english
prompt: Write a short, simple poem for young children using easy rhyming words about a friendly cat playing in the garden. The poem should have 6 lines and a cheerful tone.

lessons.title: Fun with Rhyming Poems
lessons.subject: English
learningObjectives: Identify rhyming words in simple poems
Recognize that poems can tell stories

keyVocabulary.definition: words that sound the same at the end
special words that tell a story with music
a place where flowers and plants grow
a pretty flying bug with colorful wings
the happy sound a cat makes

lessons.warmUp: Let's clap and say words that rhyme! Cat-hat-mat! Dog-log-frog! Garden-pardon! When I say a word, you say one that rhymes back to me. Ready to play the rhyming game?
lessons.mainExplanation: Poems are special word pictures that sound musical! They tell stories about people, animals, and places. When words rhyme, they sound the same at the end, like cat and hat. Poems about friendly animals make us smile and feel happy inside.
workedExamples:
POEM 1 — Fluffy's Garden Day:
Fluffy cat loves to play
In the garden every day
Chasing butterflies so bright
Rolling in the morning light
Purring soft and feeling free
Happy as a cat can be

POEM 2 — Sunny Garden Fun:
Little cat runs here and there
Sunshine dancing in her hair
Flowers smell so sweet and nice
Pouncing on the garden mice
Soft green grass beneath her paws
Playing till the day is done

easy.question: What is the cat's name in the first poem? _____
easy.hint: Look at the first line of the poem

easy.question: Match the words that rhyme: day - play, bright - light, free - be
easy.hint: Find words that sound the same at the end

easy.question: What does Fluffy chase in the garden? _____

medium.question: Complete this line from the poem: 'Purring soft and feeling _____'
medium.hint: Think about how happy cats feel

medium.question: Match the actions with what the cats do: pouncing - garden mice, chasing - butterflies, rolling - morning light
medium.hint: What does each cat do in the poems?

challenge.question: Write one sentence about what Fluffy does in the garden.
challenge.hint: Think about all the fun things Fluffy does outside

quiz.question: What is the cat's name in the first poem?
quiz.explanation: The first poem is about 'Fluffy cat' who loves to play in the garden.

quiz.question: Where does Fluffy like to play?
quiz.explanation: Fluffy cat loves to play 'in the garden every day.'

quiz.question: What does Fluffy chase in the garden?
quiz.explanation: Fluffy is 'chasing butterflies so bright' in the garden.

quiz.question: What sound does the cat make when happy?
quiz.explanation: The poem says the cat is 'purring soft and feeling free.'

quiz.question: What does the little cat pounce on?
quiz.explanation: In the second poem, the cat is 'pouncing on the garden mice.'

lessons.teacherNotes: Read each poem slowly and have children clap along with the rhythm. Repeat the rhyming words together and ask children to point out which words sound alike. Use actions like pretending to chase butterflies or roll in grass.
lessons.additionalResources: Picture books with simple rhyming poems, audio recordings of children's poetry, rhyming word cards for practice
lessons.description: Children will listen to fun poems about a friendly cat and learn about rhyming words. They will practice finding words that sound the same at the end.

resourceSections.teacherGuide:
**Fun with Rhyming Poems Lesson Guide (30 minutes)**

**Opening Circle Time (5 minutes)**
Gather students in a circle on the carpet. Show enthusiasm and speak in a sing-song voice: 'Today we're going to listen to special word pictures called poems! They sound like music and tell stories about friendly animals.' Clap hands rhythmically while saying this to build excitement.

**Introduction to Rhyming (8 minutes)**
Explain: 'Words that rhyme sound the same at the end. Let me show you!' Say 'cat' and 'hat' slowly, emphasizing the '-at' sound. Have children repeat after you. Try more pairs: 'dog/frog', 'bee/tree', 'pig/big'. Ask children to clap when they hear words that sound the same at the end. Practice with 3-4 word pairs, having students echo the rhyming sounds.

**First Poem Reading (7 minutes)**
Read a simple animal poem with clear rhymes (like 'Hickory Dickory Dock' or 'Baa Baa Black Sheep'). Read slowly and expressively, pausing at rhyming words. Ask: 'Did you hear words that sounded the same?' Point out the rhyming pairs. Read the poem again, having children raise their hands when they hear rhyming words.

**Interactive Rhyme Game (5 minutes)**
Say: 'I'll say a word about an animal, you help me find a word that rhymes!' Start with simple pairs: 'I see a cat wearing a ___' (hat), 'A little mouse lives in a ___' (house). Celebrate all attempts, even if not perfect rhymes. Use gestures and animal sounds to make it engaging.

**Second Poem and Movement (3 minutes)**
Read another short animal poem with actions (like 'If You're Happy and You Know It' with animal variations). Have children act out the animals while listening to the rhymes. This combines physical movement with listening skills.

**Closing Discussion (2 minutes)**
Sit back in circle. Ask: 'What made the poems sound special?' 'Which animals did we hear about?' 'What words sounded the same?' Praise their listening and participation. End by saying: 'Poems are word pictures that make us feel happy!'
resourceSections.teacherNotes:
**Practical Teaching Tips:**
• Use exaggerated facial expressions and hand gestures when emphasizing rhyming sounds - prekindergartners respond well to visual and physical cues
• If children suggest words that don't quite rhyme, praise the attempt and gently model the correct rhyme: 'Good try! Dog and log sound similar at the end'
• Keep poems very short (4-8 lines) as attention spans are limited - repeat poems multiple times rather than introducing too many new ones
• Have children sit close together on the carpet to maintain engagement and make it easier to hear responses
• Some students may focus more on the animal content than the rhyming - this is developmentally appropriate, so celebrate both observations

**Common Student Responses to Expect:**
• Children may identify any similar-sounding words as rhymes (like 'cat' and 'car') - acknowledge their listening effort while modeling the correct rhyme
• Some students may get excited about the animals and want to share personal pet stories - briefly acknowledge then redirect to the rhyming focus
• A few children may not respond verbally but will show engagement through body language, clapping, or smiling - this is perfectly normal participation for this age

textbookPages.title: Welcome to Poetry Time!
textbookPages.body:
Look at this big, colorful book! Today we're going to read special words called poems. Poems are like word pictures that sound like music. They tell us stories about animals, people, and fun places.

See the friendly cat on this page? Her name is Fluffy, and she loves to play in her beautiful garden. When we read poems about Fluffy, we'll hear words that sound the same at the end. These are called rhyming words!

Get ready to listen carefully and clap your hands. We're going to have so much fun with our rhyming poems!
bullets: Some words rhyme - they sound the same at the end
activities: Point to Fluffy in the picture and say her name
textbookPages.figureCaption: Fluffy the cat playing happily in her sunny garden

textbookPages.title: What Are Rhyming Words?
textbookPages.body:
Rhyming words are words that sound the same at the end. Listen carefully: CAT and HAT both end with the same sound - 'at'! When you say them together, they sound like music.

Let's try more rhyming words together. Can you hear how these words sound the same at the end? DOG and LOG both end with 'og'. BEE and TREE both end with 'ee'. When we find words that rhyme, we can clap our hands!

In our poems today, we'll find lots of rhyming words. Every time you hear words that sound the same at the end, you can point them out and clap!
bullets: Rhyming words sound the same at the end
activities: Clap when you hear rhyming words
textbookPages.callout: Listen for words that sound alike!
textbookPages.figureCaption: Pictures showing cat/hat, dog/log, and bee/tree pairs

textbookPages.title: Fluffy's Garden Day
textbookPages.body:
Here is our first poem about Fluffy the cat. Listen carefully as your teacher reads each line. Can you hear the words that rhyme?

Fluffy cat loves to play
In the garden every day
Chasing butterflies so bright
Rolling in the morning light
Purring soft and feeling free
Happy as a cat can be

Did you hear the rhyming words? 'Play' and 'day' sound the same at the end! 'Bright' and 'light' rhyme too! And 'free' and 'be' make another rhyming pair.
activities: Clap when you hear rhyming words
Pretend to chase butterflies like Fluffy
textbookPages.figureCaption: Fluffy chasing colorful butterflies in a sunny garden

textbookPages.title: What Does Fluffy Do?
textbookPages.body:
Let's talk about what happens in our poem! Fluffy is a happy cat who loves spending time in her garden. Every single day, she goes outside to play and have fun.

What does Fluffy do in the garden? She chases pretty butterflies that flutter around the flowers. She rolls around in the warm, bright morning sunshine. The soft grass feels good under her paws!

When Fluffy is happy, she makes a special sound called purring. Cats purr when they feel safe and loved. Fluffy purrs because she feels free and joyful in her beautiful garden.
bullets: Fluffy plays in the garden every day
activities: Make a purring sound like Fluffy
Roll gently on the carpet like Fluffy
textbookPages.callout: Purring means a cat is happy!
textbookPages.figureCaption: Happy Fluffy rolling in green grass under the morning sun

textbookPages.title: Sunny Garden Fun
textbookPages.body:
Now let's read another poem about a different cat having fun in the garden. Listen for the rhyming words in this poem too!

Little cat runs here and there
Sunshine dancing in her hair
Flowers smell so sweet and nice
Pouncing on the garden mice
Soft green grass beneath her paws
Playing till the day is done

This poem has rhyming words too! Can you find them? 'There' and 'hair' sound the same at the end. 'Nice' and 'mice' rhyme together. And 'paws' goes with... can you think of another word that sounds like 'paws'?
activities: Run in place like the little cat
Sniff the air like you're smelling flowers
textbookPages.figureCaption: A playful cat running through flowers in a sunny garden

textbookPages.title: Two Cats, Two Gardens
textbookPages.body:
Now we've read about two different cats! Both cats love playing in gardens, but they do different things. Let's compare what each cat likes to do.

Fluffy likes to chase butterflies and roll in the morning light. She purrs when she feels happy and free. The other little cat runs around everywhere and pounces on tiny garden mice. Both cats love the soft green grass under their paws!

Both poems tell us that cats are happiest when they can play outside. Gardens are wonderful places for cats to explore, run, and have fun. The sunshine makes everything bright and cheerful!
bullets: The little cat pounces on mice
activities: Act out chasing butterflies or pouncing
textbookPages.figureCaption: Both cats playing happily in their garden homes

textbookPages.title: Let's Find All the Rhymes!
textbookPages.body:
Now that we've read both poems, let's find all the rhyming words together! Remember, rhyming words sound the same at the end.

From Fluffy's poem: play/day, bright/light, free/be. Can you say these pairs out loud? Listen to how they sound the same at the end!

From the second poem: there/hair, nice/mice. These words rhyme too! When we put rhyming words in poems, they make the poems sound like songs.

Can you think of other words that rhyme with 'cat'? How about 'hat', 'mat', or 'bat'? Rhyming is fun to do!
activities: Say each rhyming pair out loud
Think of words that rhyme with 'cat'
textbookPages.callout: Rhyming makes poems sound like music!
textbookPages.figureCaption: Word pairs showing all the rhymes from our poems

textbookPages.title: We Love Poetry!
textbookPages.body:
What a wonderful time we've had reading poems together! We learned that poems are special because they tell stories and have words that rhyme. Rhyming words sound the same at the end, like cat and hat.

We met Fluffy, who loves chasing butterflies in her garden. We also read about another little cat who runs around and pounces on mice. Both cats are happy when they play outside in the sunshine!

Poetry is all around us. We can find poems in books, hear them in songs, and even make up our own! Remember to listen for rhyming words whenever you hear a poem.
bullets: Poems tell stories with rhyming words
We met two happy garden cats
Rhyming words sound the same at the end
Poetry is fun to listen to
activities: Tell someone about your favorite cat
Listen for rhymes in songs you know
textbookPages.figureCaption: Children reading poetry books with happy cats on the covers

poemSummaries: Fluffy the cat has a wonderful time playing in the garden every single day. She chases pretty butterflies and rolls around in the warm morning sunshine. The poem shows how happy and free Fluffy feels when she's outside playing.

A little cat runs around everywhere in her sunny garden. She smells the sweet flowers and playfully chases tiny mice. The poem tells us about her fun day playing on the soft green grass until it gets dark.
